Across the road in the fields west of the wetlands proper. I was able to get a clear look from the western viewing platform (standing on the bench may be necessary for the other short kings out there). There is a spot to pull over on the side of the road, as I saw another birder/photographer do, however, I would **NOT** recommend this, as the vegetation creates a significant blind spot for and of oncoming traffic. As noted above, the bird is visible from the wetlands itself.

description:
Found earlier today by Jim & Gary. Pics.

https://ebird.org/checklist/S131615870

Found earlier by Janet Kelly, but I believe it to be a LBCU rather than WHIM. While I'm not super confident at telling the two species apart by distant sight, it flew over fairly close and vocalized while I was observing. It made a rising "cooLI" call typical of the curlew. It was also very large in flight, with warm cinnamon tones and heavy wingbeats. It was initially in the elevated causeway between the first and second ponds to the east, but flew overhead and took off to the north out of sight. Pics and audio to follow.

description: I was at a Sniffspot with my dog when I saw a murmuration of large birds with white backs, white bellies, and dark undersides on their wings far off in the distance.  I watched them for about 10 minutes, as they flew around— I’ve seen murmurations of smaller songbirds before, which flow and reshape quickly, but never of birds this size, which seemed to wheel and turn relatively slowly, alternating between flapping and gliding. I wondered if they might be some type of crane, but I couldn’t hear any calls. Eventually they stopped murmuring and formed a V and flew over the field I was in, then began a new murmuration over the trees just beyond the field. As they were passing overhead, I was surprised to see that they looked like pelicans, with their heads tucked in and long bulky pointed beaks. They wheeled in their new murmuration for another 10-15 minutes before forming another V and flying away.

description: A very beautiful and interesting sighting at TRNWR. This group of white birds was very gently swaying in the sky as a group, circling just enough to stay in motion while hovering over nearly the same spot.  It was a gentle gliding motion that seemed like a dance.  Depending on which way they turned and the viewing angle, they went from very prominent to barely visible.  They slowly glided higher and higher until they were no longer visible by eye or camera, seeming to disappear off into the ether.   It was magical, peaceful, and beautiful to witness.

description:
This pair seemed to be touching/rubbing bills at some points (not photographed because I observed it through a spotting scope rather than a camera)

There were at least four individuals active around the "nose" of Face Rock, alternately perching and diving off the rock to fly a circuit then come back and land again. At times, they associated as two separate pairs in different places, and at other times they landed on the same ledge and seemed to associate all together.
